name: Autoboxing Integer cache in Java
question_text:
Consider:

Integer a = 127;
Integer b = 127;
Integer c = 200;
Integer d = 200;
System.out.println((a == b) + " " + (c == d));

What does this print on a typical JVM with default settings?
general_feedback: Autoboxing goes through Integer.valueOf, which caches boxed Integers in the range −128 to 127. Values inside the cache share the same Integer instance, so a == b is true. 200 is outside the cache, so c and d are distinct objects and c == d is false.
correct_feedback: Correct — 127 is inside the Integer cache (−128..127); 200 is outside, so distinct Integer objects are boxed.

answers.feedback: 127 is inside the Integer cache (−128..127); 200 is outside, so distinct Integer objects are boxed.
200 is not cached, so boxing twice creates two different Integer instances.
127 is inside the default Integer cache, so boxing both times returns the same instance.
Both outcomes are reversed from what the cache semantics produce.
